academic_profile: 3.6 unweighted GPA, trending up (3.8 junior year so far). 1240 PSAT, taking the SAT in the fall, realistic target around 1300. Two APs so far (Gov, Stats), taking AP Econ senior year.
academic_strengths: Math when he cares. Genuinely good with people, natural presenter. His DECA pitch made the district final.
academic_weaknesses: English essays are a grind. Coasted freshman and sophomore year and the GPA shows it.
extracurriculars: Varsity baseball (right field, two years), DECA (district finalist, marketing event), works 12 hrs/week at Chipotle, intramural basketball
interests: Business, sales, sports. Runs a small sneaker resale side thing with a buddy that actually makes money. Follows the stock market casually.
personality: Easygoing, well-liked, more capable than his transcript suggests. Needs a reason to try hard and then he does.
teacher_quote: His Stats teacher said Jake explains concepts to other kids better than most teachers could.
parent2_profession: Administrative coordinator at a middle school
relocation: Prefers Midwest, within a day's drive
assets: Own home, ~$150K equity. About $30K saved for college.
college_budget: $25,000 per year. Could go a little higher for something clearly worth it, but we are not doing $60K a year for a business degree.
size_preference: Big school, he wants the full college experience
must_haves: Legit business program, alumni network that helps with jobs, big-school atmosphere
deal_breakers: Crushing debt. Schools where he'd be bored.
priority_other: He's a good kid who'll rise to the level of his surroundings. We want a school that pulls him up, not just one that admits him.
additional_context: The GPA trend matters here: 3.3 freshman year, 3.6 sophomore, 3.8 this year. Does that trajectory count for anything? Also want to understand Kelley direct admit vs. standard admission at Indiana, and whether Ohio State's Fisher is realistic in-state.
